title: Add coupon discount support to charge endpoint
summary: The two genuine blockers survive: c11 (discount_amount always reports 0 because payload.amount is mutated to the discounted value before the subtraction) and c24 (PCI-DSS violation — full request body with card.number/cvc logged to console). Both stay 'must'. I collapsed the heavily duplicated panel noise: all request-body debug-logging findings (c5/c12/c15) fold into the PCI cluster c24; the unknown-coupon debug log (c13) folds into c6; the empty-catch trio (c1/c14/c25) folds into c14; the `any`-cast pair (c0/c21) folds into c0; the naming findings (c16/c17/c18) fold into the broad c7; and the ==/=== pair (c9/c19) folds into c19. I downgraded c0 (any cast) and c14 (empty catch) from must to should — real quality issues but not blockers. Everything else (YAGNI strategy pattern c2, abstract-vs-interface c3, dead code c4, var usage c10, theData intermediate c8, snake_case response c20, missing tests c23, silent invalid-coupon acceptance c22) is kept as-is. No clusters dropped — all are true of this diff.

findings.title: Using `: any` to disable type safety on response
findings.message: Line 33 uses `: any` to bypass TypeScript type checking so arbitrary fields can be added to the transaction response. This is an escape hatch that defeats the type system. Instead, model a proper response type (e.g., `interface ChargeResponse extends Transaction { discountAmount: number; coupon_code?: string }`) and construct it explicitly. This prevents errors from creeping in later.

findings.title: Strategy pattern and factory for six static rows (YAGNI)
findings.message: This file defines an abstract base class, two strategy subclasses, and a factory to handle exactly six hardcoded coupons. The PR justifies this as 'extensible for date-bound or customer-specific coupons later,' but that's speculative generality — you're building architecture for a hypothetical problem. When the actual second type of coupon exists, the shape will be obvious and refactoring will take an hour. For now, use a simple data-driven approach: const COUPONS = { WELCOME10: { type: 'percent', value: 0.1 }, ... }; then one function applies them. Much easier to understand, no inheritance, no factory.

findings.title: Abstract class with only one method should be an interface
findings.message: `AbstractCouponStrategy` has no shared concrete behavior, only a single abstract method `apply()`. This is exactly what an interface is for. Using abstract class forces single inheritance and adds zero benefit. Change to `interface CouponStrategy { apply(amount: number): number; }` and let implementations use `class X implements CouponStrategy`.

findings.title: Commented-out dead code
findings.message: Lines 46–48 contain a commented-out branch for 'FREESHIP'. Commented-out code rots and confuses readers about whether it's a plan, a rollback, or a fossil. Git remembers the history; delete it. If FREESHIP needs to come back, restore from history with full context.

findings.title: Debug console.log left in code
findings.message: `console.log('DEBUG: unknown coupon code:', couponCode)` should be removed or use a proper logger. Debug logging in production impacts observability and pollutes logs.

findings.title: Poor function and variable names
findings.message: `doStuff` is a meaningless function name (line 51). Use `applyCoupon` or `calculateDiscount` instead. Similarly: `tmp` (line 56) → `strategy`, `result2` (line 62) → `discountedAmount`. In couponDiscount.ts also: `p` (line 12) → `percentage`, `x` (line 15) → `discountedAmount`, `amt` (line 23) → `fixedAmount`. Bad names force readers to hunt through the code to understand what's happening.

findings.title: Unnecessary intermediate variable `theData`
findings.message: `const theData = req.body; ... const couponCode = theData.couponCode;` is awkward. Either extract couponCode directly (`const { couponCode } = req.body`) or just use `req.body.couponCode`. This adds no value.

findings.title: Use const/let instead of var
findings.message: `var x = ...` (line 15) and `var tmp = ...` (line 56) use `var`, which is function-scoped and can be reassigned unexpectedly. Use `const` (or `let` if reassignment is needed). Const is the default in modern TypeScript.

findings.title: discount_amount always 0 due to mutated payload.amount
findings.message: Line 24 mutates `payload.amount = finalAmount`, which stores the *discounted* amount. Then line 38 calculates `response.discount_amount = payload.amount - finalAmount`, which is `discounted - discounted = 0`. The response always reports zero discount even when a coupon was applied. Fix: save the original amount before any modifications: `const originalAmount = payload.amount;` at line 18, then use it in the response: `response.discount_amount = originalAmount - finalAmount;`

findings.title: Empty catch block silently swallows errors
findings.message: The try/catch around `doStuff` has only a TODO comment. If coupon application fails—or if the function is later extended to throw—the error is silently dropped. The customer loses their requested discount with no indication of failure, and the full amount is charged. Either handle the error explicitly (log it, return an error response) or rethrow it. Do not have empty catch blocks.

findings.title: Use of == instead of === for equality checks
findings.message: Lines 34–43 and 58 use `==` for string and null comparisons. Use strict equality `===` for consistency with TypeScript best practices and to match the codebase style (eqeqeq rule). Example: `if (code === 'WELCOME10')` and `if (tmp === null)`.

findings.title: Naming convention drift: response uses snake_case instead of camelCase
findings.message: The response includes `discount_amount` and `coupon_code` in snake_case, but the rest of the API (e.g., `customerId`, `transactionId`) uses camelCase. This mixes conventions and forces consumers to special-case your endpoint. Use `discountAmount` and `couponCode` to match the house style.

findings.title: Invalid coupon codes silently accepted without feedback
findings.message: When a coupon code is not found, the function logs a debug message and returns the original amount. The customer receives no error response — they believe they applied a discount but get charged the full amount anyway. Either validate the coupon code at the boundary (return 400 if invalid) or include an error field in the response so the client knows the coupon was rejected. Silently falling back to no discount is dishonest.

findings.title: No unit tests for coupon math or edge cases
findings.message: The PR explicitly notes '[ ] Add unit tests for the coupon math (TODO follow-up)'. Money-related logic must have tests covering: valid percentage discounts, valid fixed discounts, invalid coupon codes, undefined coupon codes, edge cases (zero amounts, negative discounts). Shipping untested discount code is risky — these cases should be proven before merge, not deferred.

findings.title: PCI-DSS violation: logging card data to console
findings.message: console.log('DEBUG charge body:', JSON.stringify(theData)) logs the entire request body, which contains card.number, card.cvc, customerId, and other payment PII. PCI-DSS explicitly forbids plaintext PAN and CVC in logs and prohibits storing CVC anywhere. The project ships a pino logger with a redaction allowlist specifically to prevent this bypass. Delete the console.log; if debugging is needed, log only non-sensitive fields (e.g., transactionId, amount) via the configured logger.
